_comment: Static knowledge layer. Port facts drawn from World Port Index (NGA, public domain) and public sources; embedded once, re-embedded only on model change.

chunks.title: Port of Piraeus
chunks.text: Piraeus is the port of Athens and the largest passenger port in Europe, handling around 20 million passengers a year. It has three main basins: the central passenger harbour (ferry gates E1-E12), the Zea and Mikrolimano marinas, and the container/cargo terminals at Keratsini-Ikonio to the west. Ferries depart from Piraeus to the Saronic islands (Aegina, Agistri, Methana, Poros, Hydra, Spetses), the Cyclades, Crete and the Dodecanese. Cruise ships berth at the dedicated cruise terminal on the south side. Position approximately 37.94 N, 23.64 E.

chunks.title: Piraeus anchorage
chunks.text: The Piraeus anchorage lies south and southwest of the port entrance, roughly around 37.90 N, 23.57 E. Tankers, bulk carriers and cargo ships wait here for a berth, bunkering, orders or crew changes. The number of vessels lying at anchor is a common indicator of port congestion. Vessels at anchor report AIS navigational status 1 (at anchor).

chunks.title: Elefsis Bay anchorage
chunks.text: Elefsis (Eleusis) Bay, north of Salamina island around 38.02 N, 23.48 E, is a sheltered anchorage and industrial port area with shipyards, oil refineries and laid-up vessels. Many older ships anchor here long-term. The bay is entered from the east past Perama or from the west.

chunks.title: Port of Aegina
chunks.text: Aegina town is the main port of Aegina island, about 15 nautical miles southwest of Piraeus (position 37.75 N, 23.43 E). It is one of the busiest domestic ferry destinations in Greece, served by conventional car ferries (about 75 minutes from Piraeus) and hydrofoils/fast catamarans (about 40 minutes). The island also has a second landing at Souvala on the north coast and heavy summer weekend traffic.

chunks.title: Port of Agistri (Skala)
chunks.text: Agistri is a small pine-covered island next to Aegina. Ferries and fast catamarans call at Skala (37.70 N, 23.36 E) and sometimes Megalochori. Sailing time from Piraeus is about 55 minutes by fast craft; many services continue from or via Aegina.

chunks.title: Port of Methana
chunks.text: Methana (37.58 N, 23.39 E) is a small spa town on a volcanic peninsula of the Peloponnese coast, called at by the conventional Saronic ferries between Piraeus, Aegina and Poros. The harbour is small and mainly serves the ferry quay and local fishing boats.

chunks.title: Port of Poros
chunks.text: Poros town (37.50 N, 23.46 E) faces the Peloponnese town of Galatas across a narrow strait. It is a major yachting hub of the Saronic with a long quay, and is served by conventional ferries (about 2.5 hours from Piraeus) and fast catamarans (about 1 hour). Small passenger boats shuttle continuously across the strait to Galatas.

chunks.title: Port of Hydra
chunks.text: Hydra (37.35 N, 23.46 E) is a car-free island; motor vehicles are prohibited and transport is by donkey, water taxi and foot. The small horseshoe harbour takes fast catamarans and hydrofoils from Piraeus (about 90 minutes) but no car ferries. The harbour is crowded in season with yachts, water taxis and day-trip boats.

chunks.title: Port of Spetses
chunks.text: Spetses (37.27 N, 23.16 E) is the outermost Saronic island, at the entrance of the Argolic Gulf. Fast catamarans and hydrofoils from Piraeus take about 2 hours, calling at Dapia harbour. Cars are heavily restricted; local traffic is scooters and water taxis. The nearby mainland landing is Kosta.

chunks.title: Port of Ermioni
chunks.text: Ermioni (37.39 N, 23.25 E) is a small Peloponnese coastal town between Hydra and Spetses, on some fast-ferry rotations from Piraeus. It has a north and a south quay and significant yacht traffic in season.

chunks.title: Port of Porto Heli
chunks.text: Porto Heli (37.32 N, 23.15 E) is a sheltered bay and resort town at the southern end of the Argolic peninsula, the end of the line for some Piraeus fast-ferry rotations via Hydra and Spetses. The wide bay is a popular yacht anchorage.

chunks.title: Salamina ferry crossing (Perama-Paloukia)
chunks.text: Salamina is connected to the mainland by a continuous shuttle ferry between Perama (37.96 N, 23.57 E) on the Attica side and Paloukia (37.97 N, 23.53 E) on the island. Open-deck car ferries cross the roughly one-nautical-mile strait every few minutes around the clock. There is no published per-sailing timetable; the service is effectively a floating bridge and one of the busiest ferry crossings in Greece.

chunks.title: Port of Elefsina
chunks.text: Elefsina (38.04 N, 23.54 E) is an industrial port in Elefsis Bay with shipyards, scrap and cargo quays, and petroleum terminals serving the nearby refineries. It handles bulk and project cargo rather than passengers.

chunks.title: Corinth Canal (Isthmia entrance)
chunks.text: The Corinth Canal cuts the Isthmus of Corinth, connecting the Saronic Gulf (east entrance at Isthmia, 37.92 N, 23.00 E) with the Gulf of Corinth. It is 6.4 km long, only about 24.6 m wide at water level and about 8 m deep, so it takes small cargo ships, yachts and tour boats but no large merchant vessels. Transits run in alternating one-way convoys; the canal closes periodically for maintenance and landslides.

chunks.title: Port of Rafina
chunks.text: Rafina (38.02 N, 24.01 E) is Attica's second passenger port, on the east coast about 25 km from Athens. Ferries and fast catamarans serve the Cyclades (Andros, Tinos, Mykonos and onward) and southern Evia. For the eastern Athens suburbs and the airport it is the closest ferry port.

chunks.title: Port of Lavrio
chunks.text: Lavrio (37.71 N, 24.06 E) is Attica's third ferry port, near Cape Sounio at the southeastern tip. It serves Kea and Kythnos in the western Cyclades plus some longer Aegean routes, and has a large marina and fishing fleet.

chunks.title: Vessel type: tanker
chunks.text: A tanker is a ship built to carry liquid cargo in bulk: crude oil, refined products, chemicals or liquefied gas. AIS ship-type codes 80-89. In the Saronic Gulf tankers are common around the Piraeus anchorage and the Elefsina/Aspropyrgos refinery terminals. A laden tanker typically cruises at 10-15 knots.

chunks.title: Vessel type: cargo ship
chunks.text: Cargo ships (AIS type codes 70-79) include container ships, bulk carriers and general cargo vessels. Container traffic in the Saronic concentrates at the Piraeus container terminal at Keratsini-Ikonio, one of the largest container ports in the Mediterranean. Typical service speed is 12-20 knots.

chunks.title: Vessel type: passenger ship and ferry
chunks.text: Passenger vessels (AIS type codes 60-69) include conventional car ferries, day-trip boats and cruise ships. Greek coastal ferries typically cruise at 14-20 knots. Cruise ships calling at Piraeus can carry several thousand passengers.

chunks.title: Vessel type: high-speed craft
chunks.text: High-speed craft (AIS type codes 40-49) are fast catamarans and hydrofoils ('Flying Dolphins') that serve the Saronic islands at 25-38 knots. They carry mostly foot passengers, are weather-sensitive, and are the fastest way from Piraeus to Aegina, Poros, Hydra and Spetses.

chunks.title: Vessel type: tug
chunks.text: Tugs (AIS type codes 31-32, 52) assist large ships when berthing and unberthing, tow barges and dead ships, and stand by for salvage. A cluster of tugs moving toward the same point can indicate an arrival, a departure or an incident. Harbour tugs are common around Piraeus, Perama and Elefsina.

chunks.title: Vessel types: pleasure craft and sailing vessels
chunks.text: Pleasure craft (AIS type 37) are motor yachts; sailing vessels (type 36) are yachts under sail. Most transmit on Class B AIS transponders, which broadcast less often and at lower power than commercial Class A. The Saronic is one of the densest yachting areas in Greece, with big marinas at Alimos, Flisvos, Zea and Lavrio and popular anchorages at Aegina, Poros, Hydra and Porto Heli.

chunks.title: Vessel type: fishing vessel
chunks.text: Fishing vessels (AIS type 30) trawl or long-line mostly at night and in the early morning. AIS navigational status 7 means 'engaged in fishing'. Small day boats fish throughout the Saronic; larger trawlers land catch at Keratsini and Salamina.

chunks.title: AIS navigational status codes
chunks.text: The AIS navigational status is set by the crew: 0 = under way using engine, 1 = at anchor, 2 = not under command, 3 = restricted manoeuvrability, 4 = constrained by draught, 5 = moored, 6 = aground, 7 = engaged in fishing, 8 = under way sailing, 15 = undefined. It is set manually and is often stale — a vessel can show 'moored' while steaming at 7 knots, so speed and position should be checked before trusting it.

chunks.title: What is AIS
chunks.text: The Automatic Identification System (AIS) is a VHF radio broadcast that ships use to announce their identity, position, course and speed. Commercial vessels over 300 gross tons must carry Class A transponders (position every 2-10 seconds under way); smaller craft often carry Class B (every 30 seconds or less often). Static data such as name, ship type, dimensions and destination is broadcast about every 6 minutes. Coverage here comes from volunteer coastal receivers, so gaps are possible.

chunks.title: What is an MMSI
chunks.text: The Maritime Mobile Service Identity (MMSI) is a nine-digit radio identity unique to each vessel, the primary key of AIS data. The first three digits identify the flag state: Greek vessels use 237, 239, 240 or 241. Unlike names, MMSIs are unambiguous; unlike callsigns, every AIS message carries one.

chunks.title: Anchoring terms
chunks.text: A vessel 'comes to anchor' or 'anchors' when it drops anchor and holds position (AIS status 1, speed near zero). It 'weighs anchor' when it raises the anchor and gets under way. 'Lying at anchor' or 'at the anchorage' means waiting — commonly for a berth, cargo orders, bunkers or weather. Anchorage congestion (how many ships are waiting) is a standard signal of port and market conditions.

chunks.title: Route: Piraeus - Aegina
chunks.text: Piraeus to Aegina is the busiest Saronic route: about 15 nautical miles, roughly 75 minutes by conventional car ferry and 40 minutes by fast catamaran or hydrofoil. Operators include Saronic Ferries (conventional) and Aegean Flying Dolphins / Alpha Lines (fast). Dozens of daily departures in summer, first sailings around 06:00-08:00.

chunks.title: Route: Piraeus - Hydra - Spetses (fast ferry line)
chunks.text: Fast catamarans run from Piraeus down the Saronic line: Poros (about 1 hour), Hydra (about 90 minutes), Ermioni, Spetses (about 2 hours) and sometimes Porto Heli. Hydra and Spetses take no car ferries, so these fast services are their lifeline. Hellenic Seaways and Alpha Lines are the main operators.

chunks.title: Route: Piraeus - Aegina - Agistri - Methana - Poros (conventional)
chunks.text: The conventional Saronic ferry rotation sails from Piraeus gate E8 to Aegina, then variously Agistri, Methana and Poros and back, carrying cars, trucks and foot passengers. Sailings run from early morning to evening. This is the slow, cheap and weather-proof complement to the fast catamarans.

chunks.title: The Saronic Gulf
chunks.text: The Saronic Gulf lies between Attica and the Peloponnese, connected to the Aegean in the southeast and to the Gulf of Corinth through the Corinth Canal in the northwest. It contains the port of Piraeus, the islands Salamina, Aegina, Agistri, Poros (with Hydra, Spetses and the Argolic coast just beyond its southern rim), major anchorages, refineries at Elefsis Bay, and some of the heaviest mixed marine traffic in Greece: ferries, tankers, container ships, cruise ships, yachts and fishing boats sharing a compact, busy sea.

chunks.title: Navigational warnings (NAVTEX / NAVWARNs)
chunks.text: Navigational warnings alert mariners to temporary hazards: firing exercises, cable and dredging work, wrecks, drifting objects, lights out of order. In Greece they are issued by the Hellenic National Hydrographic Service and broadcast via NAVTEX stations (Iraklio, Kerkyra, Limnos). Warnings carry coordinates and remain in force until cancelled.
